title: Pregnancy Test

jokes.text: Alexis describes her first day at community college as 'Season five Carrie Bradshaw, super professional and polished, but also chill and flirty in an impossibly expensive sort of way.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is getting ready for her first day at Elmdale College, a humble community college.
jokes.punchline: She frames the occasion with grandiose, fashion-magazine-level aspirational language, completely mismatched to the setting.
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential Alexis — her total inability to scale her self-image to actual circumstances is the engine of her character comedy. The specific 'Season five' qualifier is a lovely detail that shows she has a whole internal mythology about Carrie Bradshaw eras. Strong character integration because no other character would say this.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going for that effortless Carrie Bradshaw vibe, you know? Like, Sex and the City but make it community college.

jokes.text: David: 'You do know that this is your first day at Elmdale College, and not Vogue, right?'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just described her community college look in extravagant Vogue-editor terms.
jokes.punchline: David flatly punctures the delusion by naming the actual, mundane destination.
jokes.explanation: David's straight-man deflation of Alexis is a reliable dynamic and this is a clean, economical version of it. 'Not Vogue' is the perfectly chosen counter-image. Good economy — one contrast does all the work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you're not getting into Vogue. You're going to community college.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? It's a reality check, not a death sentence.

jokes.text: Johnny announces 'Kids, it's garbage day,' and Moira immediately replies 'Oh good, ours is full.'
jokes.setup: Johnny makes a mundane domestic announcement expecting compliance.
jokes.punchline: Moira responds as if 'our garbage is full' is a satisfying resolution to the matter, oblivious to the implication that she should do anything about it.
jokes.explanation: Moira's logical short-circuit — interpreting 'garbage day' purely as information rather than a call to action — is sharp character comedy. Her patrician remove from domestic chores is perfectly crystallized in three words. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So if only your top students go to Elmdale, that means you're a terrible teacher.

jokes.text: David's excuse for not taking the garbage out: 'I like, just put something in it that made it full, though.' Then Alexis: 'Yeah, and you're already wearing your little gloves, though.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tells the kids to tie up the garbage bags.
jokes.punchline: David tries to justify inaction by claiming credit for filling the bag, and Alexis points out he's already gloved up — meaning he has literally no excuse left.
jokes.explanation: The gloves detail is a nice visual punchline that exposes David's elaborate avoidance. The double beat — David's non-logic followed by Alexis catching him — works well structurally. Both siblings are equally self-serving, which is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Can someone take out the garbage?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, you're already wearing cleaning gloves.

jokes.text: David: 'This is the same college where Malala gave that devastating commencement speech, right?' Alexis: 'I don't know, David, maybe he did.'
jokes.setup: David is teasing Alexis about going to a humble community college.
jokes.punchline: David name-drops Malala (absurdly linking a Nobel laureate to a small-town community college), and Alexis doesn't recognize who Malala is, casually gendering her incorrectly.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent two-layer joke. David's dig is already good (implying Elmdale College is beneath Alexis's pretensions by associating it with a global icon), but Alexis's response is the real gem: she doesn't know who Malala is AND casually misgenders her with 'maybe he did.' This tells us everything about both siblings in eight words. High originality, perfect economy, exceptional character integration. One of the episode's best.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you hear that Malala is speaking at the college?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Maybe he did.

jokes.text: Johnny holds up a pregnancy test and Moira says 'Why are you waving a thermometer in my face?' Johnny: 'It's a pregnancy test.' Moira: 'Ewww! What?!'
jokes.setup: Johnny has found what he believes to be a shocking discovery — a positive pregnancy test.
jokes.punchline: Moira doesn't recognize a pregnancy test, identifying it as a thermometer. Her 'Ewww!' on learning what it is adds a second beat.
jokes.explanation: The thermometer misidentification is a great Moira moment — she is so removed from ordinary domestic objects she doesn't know what a pregnancy test looks like. Her visceral 'Ewww!' once corrected is perfectly in character. Clean two-beat structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm checking my temperature.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's a pregnancy test.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I know what it is.

jokes.text: Johnny and Moira's brief exchange: 'Where'd Alexis go?' / 'She went for a run, apparently there are some really big changes happening in her life right now.' / 'Yeah, I should say so.'
jokes.setup: The audience knows Moira is holding a pregnancy test. Johnny reports Alexis's excuse about 'big changes.'
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Yeah, I should say so' lands with heavy dramatic irony — she's implying the pregnancy, not Alexis's college enrollment.
jokes.explanation: Efficient dramatic irony setup. Works well as a scene transition joke. Rewatch value because the dramatic irony compounds once you know the full misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's positive.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course it's positive. I don't do negative. It's not in my vocabulary.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, it's a smiley face, so I'm assuming it's a positive result.' Moira: 'Well, that's presumptuous.'
jokes.setup: The pregnancy test shows a smiley face.
jokes.punchline: Moira challenges the interpretation of a smiley face as 'positive,' as if this is a matter genuinely open to debate.
jokes.explanation: 'That's presumptuous' is a beautifully Moira word to apply to a smiley face pregnancy test. It reveals her tendency to contest anything, even irrefutable visual evidence, with theatrical skepticism. Economy is excellent — one word does heavy lifting.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. How many children are we talking about here? Because I'm not prepared to support more than one.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Are we 100% sure it's hers?' Johnny: 'Well, this test is 100% sure it's someone's!'
jokes.setup: Moira grasps for an alternative explanation — maybe the test isn't Alexis's.
jokes.punchline: Johnny points out the test is definitively pregnant — regardless of whose it is, someone is definitely pregnant.
jokes.explanation: This functions as ironic foreshadowing — the test IS indeed someone else's (Jocelyn's). On rewatch the line lands twice as hard. Johnny's exasperated logic is well-structured. Good economy with the parallel '100%' construction.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I think Alexis knows that this is a mistake, and she's just... she's just waiting for someone to say it out loud.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Oh, are you serious? I was ready to stop after one!'
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira are reacting to the news that (they believe) Alexis is pregnant.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's first instinct is to lament that he was 'ready to stop' after one child — a darkly selfish parental admission.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Johnny is generally the 'reasonable' Rose — so hearing him say he wanted to stop at one child is a funny, human crack in that facade. It's dark but not mean-spirited, and it's earned by the show's established family dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Of course, of course. I'm here for you, sweetheart. Whatever you need to talk about, I'm all ears.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Although, I have to say, the timing is a bit much. Your mother and I were just about to have a moment, and frankly, we're exhausted from managing everyone else's problems. But go ahead.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Someone needs to talk to her!' David: 'Well, don't look at me, I'm pretty sure she already knows it's a mistake!'
jokes.setup: The family agrees someone must speak to Alexis about the pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: David deflects responsibility with the blunt implication that Alexis is already aware the pregnancy is a mistake — showing his complete refusal to engage emotionally.
jokes.explanation: David's self-protective detachment from any emotional labor is well-characterized. 'She already knows it's a mistake' is both callous and weirdly logical, which is the sweet spot for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We'll send her to one of those homes. You know, for unwed mothers. They still exist, don't they?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, it's 2020.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, the nuns will know what to do. They always do.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Yes, I'll tell her she has no right to lay this financial and psychological burden on us!'
jokes.setup: Moira volunteers to talk to Alexis about the pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: Her version of 'support' is immediately framed as an accusation — she plans to scold Alexis for burdening the family.
jokes.explanation: Moira's version of maternal support being an aggressive financial grievance is perfectly in character. The pomposity of 'financial and psychological burden' applied to her own daughter is vintage Moira. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm hearing that you're feeling unsupported, and I want to validate that. But I also think it's important that we create a space where we can all feel comfortable expressing our needs without judgment.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Now she has to move into one of those homes for unwed mothers!' / 'Oh, I'm sure there are plenty of them on this side of the tracks. Let the nuns take care of the little stranger!'
jokes.setup: The family is spiraling about what to do about Alexis's supposed pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: Moira leaps immediately to a Victorian-era solution — unwed mothers' homes and nuns — as if this is a reasonable 2018 suggestion. 'The little stranger' is a deliciously cold way to refer to a potential grandchild.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best Moira moments. The anachronism of suggesting an unwed mothers' home is absurdist in modern context, but it's completely plausible coming from Moira. 'The little stranger' as a descriptor for her own potential grandchild is memorably cold and quotable. The phrase 'this side of the tracks' signals she's already resigned to the social shame. High originality and character integration — only Moira could say this.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what? I didn't realize that my primary responsibility in this family was to be Alexis's... what, her contraceptive? Because I have to tell you, that's not a job I'm particularly qualified for, and frankly, it's not one I'm interested in.

jokes.text: David: 'I am not in a place right now to be emotionally available to a baby.'
jokes.setup: The family is discussing how to support Alexis through her supposed pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: David frames his failure to support his sister in therapy-speak — 'not in a place to be emotionally available' — as if his own emotional state is a legitimate competing concern.
jokes.explanation: David weaponizing self-care language to avoid family responsibility is both recognizable and sharply character-specific. The word 'baby' at the end — applied to Alexis, not just the hypothetical child — adds a double meaning. Highly quotable. One of David's better lines in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't apply my own eye serum. My hands shake when I'm emotionally compromised, and frankly, I'm always emotionally compromised.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'You spend a lot of time with Alexis, you could've done a better job looking out for her!' David: 'Okay, I'm sorry I'm not a condom.'
jokes.setup: Johnny blames David for not 'looking out' for Alexis — implying David should have prevented the pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: David takes the implication to its logical extreme: 'I'm sorry I'm not a condom.'
jokes.explanation: The best line of the episode. Johnny's vague accusation ('looking out for her') leaves an opening, and David drives a truck through it with perfect comic logic. The literalization of the metaphor is unexpected and immediately obvious in retrospect — that's the hallmark of a great joke. Maximum economy, high originality, deeply character-specific (David's talent for absurdist literalism). This is the kind of line that gets quoted in think-pieces about the show. Comparable in craft to the '8.0 anchor' — slightly higher given the originality and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Do you have bath salts?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, we do.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Aaaah! I always knew this would happen!'
jokes.setup: David's condom line lands amid the family chaos.
jokes.punchline: Moira's melodramatic wail — 'I always knew this would happen!' — is absurd because she clearly did not know this would happen (they only found the test moments ago), but she claims prophetic authority anyway.
jokes.explanation: Moira retrofitting herself as a Cassandra figure in real time is funny and character-consistent, but it's a lighter joke following a very strong one. The contrast with David's line makes it feel like a coda rather than a standalone moment.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Do you think you'll make it through this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know. I really don't know. I mean, Alexis is pregnant, and I'm going to be an uncle, and I just... I don't think I have the emotional capacity to handle something like that.

jokes.text: Johnny confessional: 'Okay, I just think I'm in a state of shock right now, you know, the news was very numbing, and I'm feeling very alone.' [Cut to Moira sitting beside him, visibly present.]
jokes.setup: Johnny delivers a confessional about feeling alone and in shock.
jokes.punchline: Moira is sitting right next to him — he is literally not alone — but he seems completely unaware of her presence, or she is providing zero emotional comfort.
jokes.explanation: The visual irony of claiming to 'feel very alone' while Moira sits beside him is a clean sight gag that also functions as a commentary on Moira's emotional availability. Works on multiple levels. The 'Mhmm' sounds from Moira imply she's offering zero actual consolation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Has anyone seen my sunglasses? I've looked everywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Moira, they're on your head.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Imagine how Alexis must feel.' Moira: 'Yeah, I haven't spoken to her.'
jokes.setup: Johnny prompts empathy for Alexis.
jokes.punchline: Moira's response is a non-sequitur that confirms she's done literally nothing despite volunteering to talk to Alexis — completely self-unaware.
jokes.explanation: A quiet but effective beat — Moira's confession that she hasn't done the one thing she said she'd do is delivered completely without guilt or irony, which is funnier than any defensive excuse would be.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I don't want to pry...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you're doing a wonderful job.

jokes.text: Patrick applying eucalyptus eye serum to David while David explains he needs a 'steady hand' because he's 'going through a lot right now.'
jokes.setup: David is being pampered by Patrick with eye serum in what appears to be the store.
jokes.punchline: David's justification — 'it requires a steady hand, and I'm going through a lot right now' — frames his self-indulgence as medical necessity, while Patrick dutifully complies.
jokes.explanation: The visual of Patrick patiently applying eye serum to David as a form of emotional support is a lovely character beat. David's medicalization of his skincare routine is very him. Patrick's quiet compliance says a lot about their dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I am absolutely outraged! A pregnancy? As if I didn't have enough on my plate. This is like showing up to brunch only to find out it's been rescheduled, except the brunch is my uterus and someone forgot to tell me!

jokes.text: Customer walks in: 'Hi, I was looking for some uh...' David: 'Yeah, we don't sell that.' [Beat.] Customer: 'Bath salts?' David: 'We actually do sell those.'
jokes.setup: A customer enters mid-sentence.
jokes.punchline: David preemptively dismisses the request before the customer even finishes — then has to walk it back when the actual request is something they do sell.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed three-beat joke. The preemptive dismissal reveals David's attitude toward customers (and perhaps the general public), the beat of silence, and then the complete reversal when bath salts turns out to be something they sell. The structure is close to perfect — setup, subversion, second subversion. David having to reverse himself adds a layer of embarrassment. One of the tightest joke constructions in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I have something to tell you all. I'm pregnant.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my god!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Congratulations?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, this is unexpected.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm the one who's pregnant.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, between Alexis getting knocked up, and us not having enough privacy to connect, I'm feeling very shaken.' Patrick: 'Do you think you're gonna make it, though?' David: 'Unclear. Unclear on whether I'm gonna make it through or not.'
jokes.setup: David lists two problems as if they are of equal magnitude: Alexis's pregnancy and his own lack of privacy with Patrick.
jokes.punchline: Patrick plays along by asking if David will 'make it' — treating David's self-pity with the seriousness of a medical prognosis. David's doubling-down ('Unclear. Unclear...') escalates the melodrama.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the equivalence David draws between a family crisis and his personal inconvenience. Patrick's gentle mock-concern ('Do you think you're gonna make it?') is warmly sarcastic. The repeated 'Unclear' shows David really leaning into his own drama.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You're having an affair.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: What? No!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you haven't told Roland. What else could it possibly be?

jokes.text: Moira visits Jocelyn and discovers her own sunglasses are on top of her head when asked if she'd seen them.
jokes.setup: Moira claims to have 'left her sunglasses behind' and asks if Jocelyn found them in her cleaning.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn points out the sunglasses are on top of Moira's head. Moira: 'And there they are.'
jokes.explanation: A classic and slightly tired physical comedy premise (forgetting things on your head), but Moira's unflappable 'And there they are. And I'm off, then.' rescues it. Her total lack of embarrassment is character-specific. The joke itself is familiar but the delivery elevates it slightly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you know, a bun in the oven is really just the Lord's way of saying the inn is full.

jokes.text: Moira: 'We're in the midst of a family crisis, if you must know!' Jocelyn: 'Well, I don't wanna pry, so...' Moira: 'Well, you've cracked me open.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn gently offers a polite out — 'I don't want to pry.'
jokes.punchline: Moira, who was desperate to share, immediately says 'You've cracked me open' — as if Jocelyn's one polite sentence constituted intensive interrogation.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully compact joke. Moira is desperate to gossip but needs the theatrical fiction that she was forced into it. 'You've cracked me open' is the perfect Moira phrase — melodramatic, vaguely physical, and completely dishonest about how eager she was to share. High character integration; exceptional economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Alexis, I want you to know that your mother and I are here for you, no matter what you decide to do about the baby.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, I'm not pregnant. I'm thinking about going back to school.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Right, right. Well, either way, we support you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but you don't need to be this involved in my college decision. I can handle it myself.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And to think when I awoke, my biggest concern was how to make that ragtime medley of yours work.'
jokes.setup: Moira is explaining how shocked she is by Alexis's supposed pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: Her point of contrast — the thing she was previously worried about — is how to make Jocelyn's 'ragtime medley work,' which is both specific and absurd as a major life concern.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'ragtime medley' is a very Schitt's Creek touch — it references ongoing storylines while serving as comic deflation. Moira framing the medley as her biggest pre-crisis concern is funny because it reveals the smallness of her actual life while she maintains her grande dame affect.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Alexis, I'm very concerned about your ability to handle this responsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, I know it seems like a lot, but I'm actually thinking about giving it up.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Giving it up? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, I mean, college just isn't really for me. I think I'm going to focus on other things.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: College? Oh thank God.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, she has no idea of the toll a baby can take on its mother, or its mother's mother.'
jokes.setup: Moira continues her rant about Alexis's supposed pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: She pivots from concern about Alexis to concern about herself — 'or its mother's mother' — making herself the primary victim of a pregnancy she's not having.
jokes.explanation: The addition of 'or its mother's mother' is a perfect, selfish pivot that makes Moira the gravitational center of her own universe. It's compact and totally character-defining. High character integration — this is fundamentally Moira's worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So... that happened.

jokes.text: Jocelyn reveals she is the one who is pregnant. Moira: 'If only.' [Then immediately switches to: 'Oh Jocelyn, oh Jocelyn! Oh, what a wonderful surprise for us all!']
jokes.setup: Moira has been ranting about 'Alexis's' pregnancy. Jocelyn reveals it was her test.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'If only' — wishing it were menopause instead of pregnancy — is immediately followed by a theatrical reversal to performative joy.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's central comedic reversal and it lands beautifully. 'If only' — Moira's first response before she processes — is the accidentally honest reaction, a beat of pure character truth before the social mask snaps back on. The speed of the pivot to 'Oh what a wonderful surprise for us all!' is the joke: Moira performing joy on demand. The structure (honesty → social performance) is near-perfect. High impact because it recontextualizes everything that came before.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Is that a hickey?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not a hickey, it's a mark. And before you say anything, yes, it's completely my fault. I was trying to be gentle, but apparently I'm not built for tenderness.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? You're the one who wanted to foolproof the whole thing!

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'I thought it was menopause that was making me late, and giving me the cravings, and morning sickness.' Moira: 'I wouldn't know.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn explains she mistook pregnancy symptoms for menopause.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'I wouldn't know' implies she has not experienced and will never experience menopause — a subtle vanity joke about her refusal to acknowledge aging.
jokes.explanation: Three words that do an enormous amount of work. 'I wouldn't know' is Moira denying the existence of her own aging process — she is above menopause conceptually. The economy is essentially perfect (10.0 is almost never warranted, but three words creating this much character revelation earns it). One of the quietest great jokes in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I can help you guys get some privacy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'll go away for the weekend.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, that's very kind of you, but I have a better solution. I'll just tell you to go away, and you'll save yourself the gas money.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'Listen, can you do me a favour, and just keep this between the two of us, because Roland doesn't know yet.' Moira: 'Oh! Oh Jocelyn, Roland's not the father?'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn asks Moira to keep the pregnancy secret since Roland doesn't know yet.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately leaps to the most scandalous possible interpretation: that Roland isn't the father.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly constructed misdirection. Jocelyn's phrasing is innocently ambiguous and Moira's interpretation is the funniest possible one. It also reveals Moira's scandal-hungry mind — she's hopeful there's drama. Jocelyn's flat 'Of course he is' is a great straight-man beat. Well-structured, high impact.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fifty dollars? I'll give you twenty dollars to leave and never come back.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Don't wait, best to rip it off like a band aid.' / 'Miracles don't take reservations.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says she hasn't found the right time to tell Roland.
jokes.punchline: 'Miracles don't take reservations' is a pompous, quasi-philosophical aphorism that Moira delivers as if it's a profound piece of wisdom — but it's essentially meaningless as practical advice.
jokes.explanation: 'Miracles don't take reservations' is a classic Moira-ism — it sounds profound, it means almost nothing in context, and she delivers it with absolute conviction. Highly quotable as a standalone line. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is very generous. What's the catch?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No catch. I just realized I spend so much time at the store anyway, and my apartment's just sitting empty, so I thought, why not let someone live there for free?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's... actually really kind of you.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it has nothing to do with the fact that I need someone to pay my rent because I've made some poor financial decisions and I'm in a lot of debt.

jokes.text: Johnny sits with Alexis for what he thinks is a pregnancy conversation. Alexis: 'Fine, a little nervous.' Johnny: 'Well, that's understandable, comes with the territory.'
jokes.setup: Johnny thinks he's having the pregnancy conversation. Alexis thinks he's talking about college.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's generic comforting language ('comes with the territory') applies to both situations, sustaining the misunderstanding.
jokes.explanation: The misunderstanding scene is well-constructed dramatic irony. 'Comes with the territory' is perfectly ambiguous. The rewatch bonus is significant because knowing the setup makes every line land harder on revisit. The structure carries this more than originality.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We should budget for prophylactics.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I knew this was gonna happen! I knew you were gonna get way too involved in this.'
jokes.setup: Alexis thinks Johnny is being over-involved in her college enrollment decision.
jokes.punchline: From Johnny's perspective, he's being appropriately concerned about a pregnancy — making Alexis's accusation of over-involvement deeply ironic.
jokes.explanation: Part of the sustained misunderstanding comedy. Less impactful as a standalone joke but contributes to the escalating confusion. The dramatic irony carries more weight than any individual line here.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So where is the quad? And the library with the, like, spiral staircases?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Oh, it's just down the hall and to the left.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, amazing. Thank you so much!

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Now... Ted, is he being supportive?' Alexis: 'I haven't even told Ted.' Johnny: 'Oh.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks if Ted (Alexis's boyfriend, a vet) knows about the situation.
jokes.punchline: Alexis hasn't told Ted either — which to Johnny sounds like a pregnant woman hiding the news from the father, but Alexis simply hasn't told her boyfriend she's going back to school.
jokes.explanation: Another layer of the misunderstanding. The 'Oh' from Johnny does good work — it registers as the concern of a father learning his daughter's boyfriend doesn't know she's pregnant. Sustained dramatic irony that builds well.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, is this... is this from The Social Network?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Yeah, we couldn't afford to do a photoshoot, so we just used a still from a movie.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I just wanna do one thing on my own!' Johnny: 'And you're sure this is the right one thing to pick?'
jokes.setup: Alexis insists on handling college enrollment independently.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's gentle question — 'And you're sure this is the right one thing to pick?' — sounds like a father questioning whether to raise a baby alone, when Alexis is just talking about registering for courses.
jokes.explanation: One of the better lines in the misunderstanding sequence. The question reads as completely reasonable from both interpretations — it's good ambiguous writing that sustains the joke without straining.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: We have a great campus, amazing professors, and incredible networking opportunities.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Actually, none of that's true. Our campus is falling apart, our professors are drunk, and nobody wants to network with us.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I know that! And not to mention, a lot of money. Why do you think I've been saving up my money from the vet's office?' Johnny: 'How long have you known about this?'
jokes.setup: Alexis explains she's been saving money for college from her job at the vet.
jokes.punchline: Johnny hears 'saving money' and 'how long have you known' — interpreting it as Alexis having secretly planned for a baby, which escalates his alarm.
jokes.explanation: The misunderstanding escalates nicely here. Johnny's 'How long have you known about this?' is a good reactive line that reads as increasingly panicked from his misframed perspective.

dialogue.speaker: College staff
dialogue.line: Even their free agenda is wrong and unusable

jokes.text: Alexis: 'You are making way too big a deal of this. If I don't like it, I'll just give it up, and move on!'
jokes.setup: Alexis is responding to Johnny's concern about college.
jokes.punchline: Her words — 'If I don't like it, I'll just give it up, and move on' — sound shockingly cavalier about a baby but are perfectly reasonable about a community college course.
jokes.explanation: The best line in the misunderstanding sequence. 'If I don't like it, I'll just give it up, and move on' is genuinely shocking when heard through the pregnancy frame — it sounds like Alexis is casually discussing giving up a baby. The dark comedy is heightened by how completely innocent Alexis is. The double meaning is maximally exploited here. Strong structure and character integration (Alexis's breezy pragmatism is totally in character).

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so if I just sign up for another class, I can get store credit?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: We don't give refunds.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, but what if I say it's for my emotional support dog?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: We don't give refunds.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What if I cry? Like, really commit to it?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: We don't give refunds.

jokes.text: Stevie tells David and Patrick the store is closed on a Friday at 1pm. David: 'Okay, is there something I can help you with?' Stevie: 'Do you give all your customers this kind of VIP treatment?'
jokes.setup: David is dismissive of Stevie's visit, treating her as an intruder.
jokes.punchline: Stevie sarcastically calls his brusqueness 'VIP treatment,' noting how bad David is at customer service.
jokes.explanation: A functional joke about David's terrible customer service instincts, made more charming by the David/Stevie dynamic. Not especially original but delivered with good energy.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: So yeah, I'm taking all my courses online now. It's great because I can study whenever I want.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Plus, I get to hand out my DJ business cards to everyone I meet.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Like, uh, you seem really cool. You should come to one of my sets sometime.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that's so sweet, but I'm actually... I have a boyfriend.

jokes.text: David covers for why the store is closed: 'Patrick and I were working on fixing a uh... a light bulb in the back.' Then Patrick appears with an obvious hickey on his neck.
jokes.setup: David is awkwardly explaining why the store is closed in the middle of the day.
jokes.punchline: Patrick emerges from the back with a hickey visible on his neck, instantly exposing the lie.
jokes.explanation: A solid visual reveal — the hickey puncturing the 'light bulb' alibi is well-timed. The addition of Patrick trying to corroborate ('David and I were just doing some inventory') while visibly hickeyed is a good layer. The visual gag is the main comedic payload here.

dialogue.speaker: College Staff Member
dialogue.line: We're here to help you with whatever you need.

dialogue.speaker: College Staff Member
dialogue.line: Have you thought about what you'd like to study? Or... are you free Friday night?

jokes.text: Stevie: 'You got a little red um... is that a bug bite? Or a little mouth shaped sunburn? Looks like a hickey, actually.'
jokes.setup: Stevie notices the mark on Patrick's neck.
jokes.punchline: Her sequence of increasingly pointed guesses — bug bite → mouth-shaped sunburn → hickey — builds with perfect comic timing. 'Mouth shaped sunburn' is the peak absurdist escalation.
jokes.explanation: 'A little mouth shaped sunburn' is genuinely funny — the specificity of 'mouth shaped' combined with the absurdity of 'sunburn' as a cover story is exactly the kind of comic escalation that Stevie does better than anyone. This is Stevie at her deadpan best. Well-structured three-step reveal with the payoff at step two before the flat naming at step three. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The mark is above the alcohol level.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, that's the whole point of drinking, isn't it? To get above the line.

jokes.text: David: 'Because that would be disgusting, and wildly unprofessional.' Then: 'David, did you give me a hickey?!' David: 'Okay no, I didn't, I gave you a half hickey, because we haven't had the time or the privacy for me to give you a full hickey!'
jokes.setup: Patrick is embarrassed by the hickey and David tries to deny it.
jokes.punchline: David's defense — he only gave a 'half hickey,' not a full one, due to privacy constraints — is both a logical absurdity and a confession that reveals more than it conceals.
jokes.explanation: The 'half hickey' taxonomy is a David Rose original — only he would create a gradated scale of hickey completion and invoke it as a defense. The logic that his privacy constraints prevented a complete hickey is absurd and perfectly specific to his character. The setup of calling it 'disgusting and unprofessional' followed immediately by his confession makes the escalation land harder. Strong originality and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Who's that?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh, that's Jake. He's picking me up.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Jake? Since when do you call him that? I thought his name was... well, I don't know his name.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's Jake. And he calls me pony.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Pony? Like the animal?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's a pet name.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's depressing.

jokes.text: Stevie offers her apartment for David and Patrick. David: 'Why? Why would you do that?' Stevie: 'Because I care about you two.' David: 'Okay, and where will you be?'
jokes.setup: Stevie offers a generous gesture — lending her apartment.
jokes.punchline: David's suspicious 'Why? Why would you do that?' — as if altruism is inherently suspicious — followed by his immediate logistical question of where Stevie will be.
jokes.explanation: David's inability to accept generosity at face value is good character comedy. The 'Why? Why would you do that?' double beat is sharp — he asks twice, as if the first time didn't adequately communicate his suspicion. The rewatch bonus comes from knowing Stevie has a hidden agenda (her date with Jake).

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh yes, because nothing says 'term of endearment' like calling someone a horse.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sure she's thrilled to be compared to a four-legged animal that lives in the woods.

jokes.text: Stevie explains she's going to 'take a little me time at a spa in Elmdale.'
jokes.setup: Stevie offers a perfectly innocent explanation for her absence.
jokes.punchline: The audience later learns she's actually going on a date with Jake — the 'spa' is a cover story.
jokes.explanation: This functions primarily as setup for the later Jake reveal. On first watch it's a mild joke; on rewatch it's a deliberate lie delivered with total composure. The rewatch value is the main asset.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait. Why are you being so nice to me? You never do this.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You're being suspiciously generous. You're hiding something. What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not hiding anything.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're dating someone, aren't you?

jokes.text: Patrick: 'That is super generous, but wouldn't it be a little bit weird, us having alone time at your place? Especially because you guys have...' Stevie/David: 'Oh God no! No, we've never... No, never, ew! We've never done that there. Never. I still live there.'
jokes.setup: Patrick raises the obvious awkward question — have David and Stevie been intimate in her apartment?
jokes.punchline: The simultaneous emphatic denials from both David and Stevie — with the specific 'I still live there' as Stevie's reason — imply it has definitely happened elsewhere, just not the apartment.
jokes.explanation: The choreography of the denials is funny — both people protest too quickly, too much. 'I still live there' as Stevie's specific objection (implying the apartment is sacred but other venues are not) is a good layered joke. Patrick's discomfort as the outsider to this history is also well-played.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm getting hungry. And you know what that means — I'm about to become unbearable. So if I say anything cutting or unnecessarily harsh in the next few minutes, just know it's the hunger talking, not my actual contempt for you.

jokes.text: David: 'I mean, we could consider it.' [Beat — referring to having used Stevie's apartment at some point]
jokes.setup: Both Stevie and David have just vehemently denied any intimacy at Stevie's apartment.
jokes.punchline: David, in a moment of self-undermining, suggests they 'could consider it' — implying the denial was provisional.
jokes.explanation: A small but effective beat. David's 'I mean, we could consider it' is a perfect non-sequitur that simultaneously embarrasses Patrick and reveals David's unembarrassed relationship with his complicated history with Stevie. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: My wife's cooking? It's like she's trying to kill me. Every night I'm wondering if this is the one that does it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I... that's... I mean, Moira is actually a very creative cook.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Creative? That's what you call it? I call it a cry for help. Last night she made something I think was supposed to be chicken.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Ha. Yes. Well. You know, I've had some of her dishes that were quite... memorable.

jokes.text: David asks 'What exactly is the catch?' Stevie says 'No catch.' [Long pause, David and Patrick look at each other.] They take the apartment. Stevie immediately takes a bottle of something from the store. David: 'So there's a bit of a catch.'
jokes.setup: Stevie insists there's no catch to borrowing her apartment.
jokes.punchline: She immediately takes something from the store (implied to be product she's taking as payment), prompting David's perfectly resigned 'So there's a bit of a catch.'
jokes.explanation: The comedic rhythm here is excellent — 'no catch,' a beat of hope, then the immediate visual contradiction. David's 'So there's a bit of a catch' is delivered with perfect defeated resignation. The structure (statement → beat → reversal → understatement) is clean and satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Roland, I'm pregnant.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Pregnant? With what?

jokes.text: Johnny's pregnancy-crisis budget planning: 'If David sells the store, and you get some part time work, and we get a good price for the car, I think we can make it through the first two years easy, and then we just have to figure out preschool.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has been crunching numbers since learning about the supposed pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: He's built out a complete two-year financial survival plan that includes selling David's business and Moira getting a job — all while Moira has already learned the truth.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Johnny's crisis planning — selling the store, the car, part-time work, preschool — reveals his competent-father anxiety in a funny light. The fact that Moira already knows the truth makes his earnestness comic. 'Selling David's store' as line item one is a nice character-specific detail.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I'm pregnant!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's great! Now, I'm thinking we order some wings. What do you want?

jokes.text: Moira: 'Alexis isn't the one who's pregnant. Someone else is, but don't press me for details, because I've been sworn to secrecy.' [Half-second pause.] 'It's Jocelyn!'
jokes.setup: Moira announces she's been sworn to secrecy.
jokes.punchline: She immediately reveals the secret. The 'sworn to secrecy' claim lasts approximately two seconds.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. The structure is near-perfect: the solemn declaration of secrecy immediately violated. The comedy lives entirely in the gap between 'don't press me' and 'It's Jocelyn!' — no gap at all. This is pure Moira: she needs the performance of discretion while being constitutionally incapable of it. High character integration, excellent economy, and it gets a huge laugh because the timing of the self-betrayal is so immediate. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: To Jocelyn and the little bun in the oven!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You're gonna be the designated driver for the next nine months.

jokes.text: Moira: 'But you're not supposed to know. She hasn't told Roland yet.' Johnny: 'Jocelyn!' Moira: 'Huh?' [Johnny points at the window/outside where Roland might be.] Moira: 'She was appropriately gobsmacked herself.'
jokes.setup: Moira has just revealed Jocelyn's secret.
jokes.punchline: She continues adding details ('She was appropriately gobsmacked herself') as if her breach of confidence is something to be elaborated on rather than stopped.
jokes.explanation: Extension of the previous joke — Moira continues to share details while technically having broken the secret. 'Appropriately gobsmacked' is a good Moira-ism. Lighter than the preceding moment but keeps the energy going.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm so happy this is happening to you both. Really, truly happy. For you. That it's happening to you.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I am entirely relieved. Yeah, that it's not Alexis!' Johnny: 'But save that spreadsheet, John, and you might want to add a line item for prophylactics.'
jokes.setup: The crisis is resolved — Alexis isn't pregnant.
jokes.punchline: Moira suggests Johnny keep his crisis spreadsheet and add 'prophylactics' as a line item — suggesting they plan better for a future theoretical Rose pregnancy.
jokes.explanation: Moira's use of 'prophylactics' — the most clinical and antiquated possible word — is perfect. It callbacks the spreadsheet joke while revealing Moira's approach to family planning is both formal (line items, spreadsheets) and completely hands-off (not her problem to actually do anything). Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: We're like a store that's closed for renovations. We need to put up the 'out of order' sign on the relationship stuff and just... focus on the romance section.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you just compare our relationship to a store?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: A very well-organized store.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I've already had a serious talk with Alexis, though now I'm wondering what exactly... we were talking about.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to take credit for the parental talk with Alexis.
jokes.punchline: He realizes mid-sentence that he has no idea what the conversation was actually about — since he thought they were discussing pregnancy and Alexis thought they were discussing college.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying button on the misunderstanding storyline. Johnny's dawning realization — delivered mid-boast — is good structural comedy. The callback to the elaborate misunderstanding scene gives this extra resonance. 'Now I'm wondering what exactly we were talking about' is a perfectly calibrated moment of self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So tell me, before me, how many serious relationships have you had?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, I'm not doing this.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? I'm just curious about your past.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: No. We're not having this conversation. I know where this goes.

jokes.text: Alexis arrives at Elmdale College and asks the person outside where the registrar's office is, expecting a 'moss covered building by the lake' like on the brochure.
jokes.setup: Alexis approaches the college with her Vogue-level expectations.
jokes.punchline: The reality is apparently a completely unremarkable building — nothing like the aspirational brochure images.
jokes.explanation: The reality vs. expectation gap continues Alexis's college storyline. The brochure detail is specific enough to be funny but the joke is a bit slowly set up. It pays off better in the next scene.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So Dad was telling me about the big business opportunity he's been working on.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? Dad doesn't have any business opportunities.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He said something about a massive... thing. He was very excited.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was talking about my colonoscopy results.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Your what?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The doctor said everything came back clear. No polyps.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So you were excited about your... colon?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm 60 years old, Alexis. A clean bill of health is a big deal.

jokes.text: Admissions worker reveals the brochure uses stock photos and actually has a watermarked still from 'Dead Poets Society.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is confused that the college doesn't look like its brochure.
jokes.punchline: The brochure used stock photos, one of which is a recognizable still from 'Dead Poets Society' — complete with watermark.
jokes.explanation: The 'Dead Poets Society' watermarked stock photo is a delightfully specific detail — the choice of that particular film (which is about academic aspiration) is an ironic meta-joke. The admissions worker's casual delivery ('We couldn't afford in-house photography') makes it funnier. Good originality.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, you need to be more supportive. What if Alexis were pregnant with twins?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would literally fake my own death.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I feel like that's false advertising, especially considering what I am paying for these courses.' Worker: 'Yeah, but you get free coffee and doughnuts.' [Beat.] 'Oh, and like, an agenda.' Worker: 'Yeah, it's off by a day. So... I wouldn't use it.'
jokes.setup: Alexis complains about false advertising in the brochure.
jokes.punchline: The worker's attempt to list compensating benefits — coffee, doughnuts, and an agenda — immediately undermines each one. The agenda is 'off by a day' and therefore useless.
jokes.explanation: The descending scale of 'benefits' — coffee/doughnuts (fine) → agenda (okay) → it's off by a day (useless) — is a well-structured escalation downward. The worker's cheerful apathy about the broken agenda is very funny. 'So... I wouldn't use it' is delivered with wonderful indifference.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, think about it. Babies are like the ultimate accessory. They're portable, they're cute, and honestly, they're a great way to seem more relatable at parties.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, no.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Plus, they're constantly changing, so it's like having a new wardrobe every few months. And the sleep deprivation? That's just free weight loss. You literally cannot eat when you're that tired.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not having a baby based on your skincare regimen logic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, they're also super affordable. I mean, once you factor in the crib, the stroller, the car seat, the clothes they immediately outgrow... it's basically like collecting art, except it screams at 3 a.m.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Okay great, I would like a refund, please.' Worker: 'Uh, the problem is, is we don't give refunds.' Alexis: 'Okay, then I guess I would just like my money back, then.' Worker: 'Again, we don't give refunds.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is disappointed with the college and wants out.
jokes.punchline: Alexis rephraes 'refund' as 'my money back' as if the wording distinction might change the outcome. The worker's flat 'Again, we don't give refunds' treats her rephrasing with complete seriousness.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully specific joke about Alexis's particular brand of entitled optimism — she genuinely believes asking for 'my money back' instead of 'a refund' is a meaningful distinction that might yield a different result. The worker's 'Again' is perfect: it acknowledges the attempted workaround while refusing to dignify it. High character integration (this is exactly how Alexis's mind works) and excellent structure.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'This is the first time that I've ever invested in anything myself, and I thought that was like, the responsible choice, but clearly, this is like, a joke school.' Worker: 'Well, that's why most students do their courses online.'
jokes.setup: Alexis delivers an emotional speech about investing in herself for the first time.
jokes.punchline: The worker's response to her earnest vulnerability is a completely practical suggestion: most students just do it online, implying the physical college is indeed a 'joke school.'
jokes.explanation: The worker's total absence of sympathy in response to Alexis's genuine emotional moment is funny — not cruel, just oblivious. The worker's agreement-by-implication that the physical campus is not worth attending validates Alexis's complaint in the most unhelpful way possible. Good character integration for Alexis's arc.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'So like, potentially, I could refund my refund request, do all my courses online, and like, never have to ever come back here again?' Worker: 'That's actually how I keep my side hustle going, part time DJ.'
jokes.setup: Alexis realizes she can just do everything online.
jokes.punchline: The worker casually reveals he also does his job online while maintaining a DJ side hustle — implying the entire college infrastructure is essentially a formality and he himself is barely there.
jokes.explanation: The worker's revelation that he runs his job remotely while doing a DJ side hustle is a funny, absurdist touch that deepens the college's general seediness. It also validates Alexis's 'joke school' assessment. The non-sequitur of 'part time DJ' is the payoff.

jokes.text: Worker: 'Here's my card, in case you have any problems, or you wanna go on a date, or something.' Alexis: 'Okay. Well, I won't be doing any of that, but thank you so much.'
jokes.setup: The worker offers his card for college-related issues.
jokes.punchline: He casually adds 'or you wanna go on a date, or something' — a workplace come-on delivered with total nonchalance. Alexis's polite but total rejection ('I won't be doing any of that') is perfectly weighted.
jokes.explanation: 'Or you wanna go on a date, or something' is perfectly casual — delivered as if it's a standard card-offering scenario. Alexis's rejection is warm but total: 'I won't be doing any of that.' The 'any of that' covering both the problems AND the date is a nice economy.

jokes.text: Stevie shows David and Patrick her apartment. 'Just so you know, there's nothing in the fridge. Oh, and I marked the booze, so I'll know if you touch it.'
jokes.setup: Stevie is being a 'generous' host and showing them around.
jokes.punchline: Her first house rule is that she's marked the alcohol level — generosity immediately followed by micromanagement and mistrust.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's house rules reveal her pragmatic self-interest beneath the generous gesture. 'I marked the booze' is specific enough to be funny — she didn't just hide it, she measured it. Good character integration.

jokes.text: David: 'I noticed the line is a couple inches above where the whiskey starts.' David: 'I am stunned by your generosity.'
jokes.setup: Stevie has marked the liquor levels.
jokes.punchline: David has already assessed the situation and noted the margin is 'a couple inches above where the whiskey starts' — meaning there is almost nothing available to them. His 'stunned by your generosity' is pure sarcasm.
jokes.explanation: David's immediate assessment of how much liquor is actually available to them is both funny (he went straight to check) and reveals his priorities. 'Stunned by your generosity' delivered after this calculation is excellent deadpan. Good callback to Stevie's house rule.

jokes.text: David asks where exactly Stevie is going. Patrick: 'She already told us, so maybe just say thank you.' David: 'Oh no, I did, I did say thank you multiple times.' Patrick: 'Um, what is the name of the spa?'
jokes.setup: Patrick is telling David to let Stevie go without interrogating her. Then Patrick immediately starts interrogating her.
jokes.punchline: Patrick corrects David for asking questions, then immediately asks his own question about the spa's name — becoming the thing he just criticized.
jokes.explanation: A well-structured hypocrisy joke. Patrick's self-contradiction happens in real time — he can't resist the same suspicious curiosity he just called David out for. The speed of the reversal is what makes it land. It also shows Patrick's underlying discomfort with the David/Stevie history without being explicit about it.

jokes.text: Jake arrives to pick up Stevie. 'Hey, pony! Are you ready to go?' Stevie: 'What are you doing? I said I would meet you outside!'
jokes.setup: Stevie is trying to sneak Jake past David (her former partner) without a scene.
jokes.punchline: Jake blows the cover immediately by walking in calling her 'pony' — an intimate nickname that detonates Stevie's careful social management.
jokes.explanation: 'Pony' as a nickname is absurdist enough to be funny on its own, but the comedic purpose is to make the situation as awkward as possible. Jake's total obliviousness to the social situation is classic situational comedy. Stevie's 'What are you doing?!' registers the size of the mistake he's made.

jokes.text: David sees Jake. Jake: 'David. Wow, it's been a while.' David: 'Pony.'
jokes.setup: David is surprised to see Jake — his ex — picking up Stevie.
jokes.punchline: David's response to Jake's casual greeting is a single flat 'Pony' — acknowledging the nickname with deadpan acknowledgment of the absurdity of the situation.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest comedic moments. David's one-word response to the chaos of his ex-boyfriend arriving to pick up his other ex contains entire volumes. 'Pony' does multiple things at once: it acknowledges Jake, it acknowledges the bizarre nickname, it communicates David's discomfort, and it demonstrates his composure. One word. Perfect economy. High character integration — only David would respond this way to this situation.

jokes.text: Jake: 'And you are?' Patrick: 'Patrick.' Jake: 'Picking up Stevie.' Patrick: 'Yeah, we're gonna go, so...' Jake: 'So I'm not getting a name, then.'
jokes.setup: Jake asks who Patrick is.
jokes.punchline: Patrick gives a minimal response that doesn't answer the social question of who he is to David. Jake's 'So I'm not getting a name, then' calls out the evasion.
jokes.explanation: The awkwardness of Patrick being unable or unwilling to explain himself to Jake is good cringe comedy. 'So I'm not getting a name, then' is a nice observation that highlights Patrick's social discomfort in this tangled situation.

jokes.text: David, addressing Jake's 'date night in the woods' plan: 'Yeah, just galloping around the woods, huh? Yeah, with your pony.'
jokes.setup: Jake says he and Stevie plan to spend the evening in the woods.
jokes.punchline: David sarcastically extrapolates the 'pony' nickname into a literal image of Stevie galloping in the woods.
jokes.explanation: David weaponizes the 'pony' nickname to needle Jake and Stevie simultaneously. The image of Stevie 'galloping around the woods' is absurd and pointed. Good callback use — David's tone conveys jealousy through sarcasm rather than confrontation, which is exactly his character.

jokes.text: David: 'So you offering your apartment had nothing to do with any guilt you felt about harboring a little secret?' Stevie: 'Nope. Just trying to be a good person, so.'
jokes.setup: David accuses Stevie of lending her apartment out of guilt over hiding her Jake relationship.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat 'Just trying to be a good person, so' — delivered while clearly lying — is a perfect deadpan denial.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's commitment to the lie with perfect composure is very much her character. 'Just trying to be a good person, so' is delivered in a way that makes it clear she knows David knows she's lying, and she doesn't care. Good economy.

jokes.text: Jake: 'Unless you two wanna come?' David: 'Nope! I don't think so.' Jake: 'Come on.' Stevie: 'You do you.'
jokes.setup: Jake casually invites David and Patrick to join them on their date.
jokes.punchline: David's immediate 'Nope!' and Stevie's dismissive 'You do you' create a moment of comic awkwardness. Jake's obliviousness to why this is strange is the heart of the joke.
jokes.explanation: Jake's genuine openness to including his exes in his date night is absurdly casual. 'Nope! I don't think so' from David registers both the absurdity of the invitation and his underlying discomfort. Good economy in the rapid-fire exchange.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'So we're gonna talk about this, right?' David: 'Yep, I'm just gonna grab that bottle of...' Patrick: 'Whiskey?' David: 'Yeah, the whiskey.'
jokes.setup: Patrick wants to process the Jake situation emotionally.
jokes.punchline: David's response is to immediately reach for alcohol — completely circumventing the emotional conversation.
jokes.explanation: David's instinct to reach for the whiskey rather than have the difficult conversation is very him — and it callbacks the marked bottle from earlier. Patrick completing his sentence ('Whiskey?') with resigned acceptance is a good partner beat. Efficient comedy.

jokes.text: Roland takes Jocelyn's Dorito casserole clue that she's pregnant. Roland: 'Well honey, you don't need a special reason for taco night.' / 'Besides wanting to keep me as your husband? Am I right, Johnny?'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is trying to hint at her pregnancy through her Dorito casserole cravings.
jokes.punchline: Roland completely misses the hint and interprets it as a nice dinner gesture, then makes a genial self-congratulatory joke ('Am I right, Johnny?') that reveals his total obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: Roland's self-satisfied 'Am I right, Johnny?' pulls Johnny into his obliviousness, which is funny because Johnny knows the real reason. Roland's charm is that his blundering is good-natured. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Jocelyn is about to tell Roland the news, and Roland says 'Oh, hold that thought, honey. Hey, Johnny, Moira, Jocelyn's just about to tell me something exciting, come and join us.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is about to share a private, intimate piece of news with Roland.
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately invites strangers to the private conversation — he's genuinely enthusiastic about sharing the moment, completely oblivious to Jocelyn's need for privacy.
jokes.explanation: A great Roland joke — he hears 'exciting news' and immediately wants to share the experience with people nearby. There's something genuinely sweet about his communal instinct even as it completely derails Jocelyn's intimate moment. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Johnny desperately trying NOT to be pulled into the conversation: 'No, no, no. No, in fact I am gonna ask again. And again. And again.' Roland: 'Get over here and sit yourself down!'
jokes.setup: Johnny knows the secret and desperately doesn't want to be present when Roland finds out.
jokes.punchline: Roland insists with increasing force — the escalating 'and again, and again' from both sides creates a comedic tug-of-war. Johnny's 'no, no, no. No, in fact I am gonna ask again' is a funny misfiring — he says the wrong thing.
jokes.explanation: A good escalation beat. Johnny's fumbled 'no, no, no... no, in fact I am gonna ask again' is a nice little error where he contradicts himself mid-sentence under pressure.

jokes.text: Jocelyn tries to hint: 'The last time I made that casserole was when I was pregnant with Mutt. And I've really been craving that casserole again.' [Long pause.] Roland: 'Well, that's understandable, honey, I mean, it's a terrific casserole. I'd kill for a casserole right now.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn draws an explicit parallel between her casserole craving and her previous pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: Roland completely processes this as a compliment to the casserole and announces he'd kill for one right now — total failure to receive the hint.
jokes.explanation: Roland's density here is perfectly calibrated. Jocelyn can't be more explicit short of saying the words, and Roland still processes it as casserole appreciation. 'I'd kill for a casserole right now' is the peak of his obliviousness — he has completely missed the point while eagerly endorsing the associated craving. Strong character integration; Roland's lovable obtuseness is a key character trait.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Rollie, how are you not getting this?' Then Johnny, unable to contain himself: 'Oh, I need to be a part of this.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has been trying to maintain distance from Jocelyn's pregnancy announcement but Roland's obliviousness is too much.
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, I need to be a part of this' — Johnny can't maintain his polite non-interference and inserts himself into the moment.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's inability to stay out of a situation he knows the answer to is a good character beat. His 'Oh, I need to be a part of this' is both honest and funny — he's too invested to pretend otherwise. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'I'm pregnant!' [Roland: stunned silence. Waitress Twyla appears.] Roland: 'We're having a baby. Hey, I'm gonna have the bottomless French Onion soup, and maybe you might wanna drop a little calamari on the table.' Johnny: 'John!' Roland: 'Yes! Oh, oh, pregnant?'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn announces the pregnancy to Roland.
jokes.punchline: Roland processes the news, then immediately pivots to ordering food with the same energy — then seems to catch up to the news. His delayed 'Yes! Oh, oh, pregnant?' lands as him needing a second lap to process.
jokes.explanation: Roland ordering food mid-moment of receiving life-changing news is a perfectly Roland thing to do. The timing of Twyla's arrival at the exact moment of the announcement is great comic staging. His 'Yes! Oh, oh, pregnant?' as the second-lap realization is beautifully played — he accepts then is surprised again. Strong character integration and structure.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Champagne for everybody, on me!' Jocelyn: 'I can't drink, Rollie.' Roland: 'Oh right, you're driving.' Twyla: '...' Roland: 'Uh, you know what Twyla, actually, let's just do one beer, one beer for me.'
jokes.setup: Roland celebrates by offering champagne to everyone.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn can't drink because she's pregnant — but Roland assumes it's because she's driving. Then, in a brilliant climbdown, he quietly cancels the champagne toast and just orders himself one beer.
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect joke. The escalating deflation — grand 'champagne for everyone,' Jocelyn's correction, Roland mishearing why she can't drink, and then quietly downgrading the celebration to 'one beer, one beer for me' — is masterfully structured. Roland's dignity in asking for just one beer for himself is both sad and hilarious. The structure (grand gesture → misunderstanding → bathetic personal consolation) is exactly how great situational comedy works. One of the two or three best jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Moira's toast: 'I can honestly say from the bottom of my heart, that I couldn't be happier that this is happening to you and your family!'
jokes.setup: Moira is offering congratulations to Roland and Jocelyn.
jokes.punchline: 'That this is happening to you and your family' — the phrasing subtly distances Moira from any involvement or shared joy. She's happy for them, entirely separate from herself.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully crafted Moira line. The warmth-that-isn't-warm of 'I couldn't be happier that this is happening to YOU and YOUR family' is everything. She phrases a toast as if it's a narrow escape from personal involvement. The inclusion of 'from the bottom of my heart' makes the distancing clause even more pointed. High originality in the specific word choice; near-perfect character integration. This is Moira at her most accidentally revealing.

jokes.text: David and Patrick make a toast to 'privacy' in Stevie's apartment with the marked whiskey bottle visible.
jokes.setup: David and Patrick finally have their private time.
jokes.punchline: They toast to 'privacy' — the episode's central theme for their storyline — while using the whiskey that Stevie measured.
jokes.explanation: A quiet button on the David/Patrick storyline. Toasting to 'privacy' is a gentle irony given how much the evening was disrupted. The implied use of the marked whiskey is a visual callback. Functional more than exceptional.

jokes.text: David processes the Jake/Stevie situation: 'So this is for you. Um... cheers to privacy.' [But then:] 'Sorry, I've just gotta go through that one more time. So you dated Jake and then Stevie dated Jake.'
jokes.setup: David and Patrick have toasted to privacy and moving on from the evening's drama.
jokes.punchline: David immediately re-opens the topic — he literally cannot lock it up — and begins methodically re-reviewing the romantic history.
jokes.explanation: David's inability to actually move on from a topic he declared closed is very funny and very him. 'Sorry, I've just gotta go through that one more time' is the confession of a person who cannot process and release. The systematic re-cataloguing ('So you dated Jake, and then Stevie dated Jake...') is a beautiful character beat.

jokes.text: David: 'And at one point, you all...' Patrick: 'No, see, that's where you're wrong. We almost all, but I said no.' David: 'Ah.'
jokes.setup: David is reconstructing the romantic history involving Jake, Stevie, and himself.
jokes.punchline: Patrick reveals there was a point where they 'almost all' were involved, but Patrick declined — implying David agreed and Patrick was the holdout.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's correction is revealing and slightly scandalous — the 'almost all' implication is the joke. His pride in having been the responsible party is also funny. David's simple 'Ah' of acceptance/understanding is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'You're upset about this because you still...' David: 'No! I'm not upset! I-I don't want any of that!' Patrick: 'No...' David: 'It's a principle thing.'
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests David is upset because he still has feelings for Stevie or Jake.
jokes.punchline: David's explosive denial — 'I'm not upset! I don't want any of that!' followed immediately by 'It's a principle thing' — reveals he absolutely still has feelings (or at least competitive feelings) about the situation.
jokes.explanation: 'It's a principle thing' is a classic deflection that means the opposite — when someone retreats to principles, it's always personal. David's over-emphatic denial is well-played. Good character integration: David's relationship to his own emotional states is consistently evasive.

jokes.text: David: 'And we are getting sidetracked right now. Okay? So... who is feeling sexy?' Patrick: 'Getting there for sure, I just-you know...'
jokes.setup: David has been deep in processing the Jake/Stevie situation.
jokes.punchline: David's abrupt pivot to 'who is feeling sexy?' — as if that can be switched on after an uncomfortable relationship archaeology session — is both self-aware and completely oblivious.
jokes.explanation: 'Who is feeling sexy?' as a mode-switch attempt after a deeply unsexy conversation is very funny. Patrick's 'Getting there for sure, I just...' trails off with the unspoken 'we literally just talked about you sleeping with two people I know.' Good character integration — David's desire to simply move forward (in more ways than one) without actually processing the emotional content.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'I knew you had a rich dating history, David, I just didn't expect to be graced by the presence of two of your exes tonight.'
jokes.setup: Patrick is processing the evening's surprise encounters with Jake and the history with Stevie.
jokes.punchline: 'Graced by the presence' — the formal, elevated language for an unwelcome surprise — is perfectly dry. The double 'two of your exes in one night' observation is the understated punch.
jokes.explanation: 'Graced by the presence' as applied to two awkward ex-encounters is a good Patricism — dry, formal, slightly wounded. It's the kind of line that shows how Patrick processes discomfort through very composed language. Good character integration.

jokes.text: David: 'You know what, we didn't even get into your history...' Patrick: 'Lock it up, David. Lock it up.' David: 'Oh, okay.'
jokes.setup: David tries to open a new thread — Patrick's romantic history — just as they've agreed to move on.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's firm 'Lock it up, David. Lock it up' — twice — is the clean and decisive shutdown of David's instinct to excavate.
jokes.explanation: 'Lock it up, David. Lock it up.' is a very quotable line that also reveals Patrick's limits — he will not allow his history to be the next excavation project. The repetition ('Lock it up. Lock it up.') is the right amount of emphasis. Good economy.

jokes.text: Alexis tells Johnny: 'I think I can find a way to make it work.' David assumes she means the pregnancy. Johnny: 'No, that's not what was happening! I-I thought we were talking about something else.' Alexis: 'What else?'
jokes.setup: The misunderstanding is coming to a resolution.
jokes.punchline: 'What else?' — Alexis has no idea what other 'something else' there could possibly be, because from her perspective it was always about college.
jokes.explanation: A good payoff beat on the misunderstanding sequence. Alexis's innocent 'What else?' — unable to conceive what other topic there could have been — is a good character-specific moment that also serves as the scene's comedic hinge before the big reveal.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, he thought you were pregnant.' Alexis: 'What? Ew!'
jokes.setup: The misunderstanding is resolved.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'What? Ew!' — her first reaction to the pregnancy assumption is disgust rather than, e.g., concern or confusion.
jokes.explanation: A mirror callback to Moira's 'Ewww! What?!' at the start of the episode — now from the other side. Alexis's instinctive disgust is very her. The economy is good. The callback structure gives it extra points even if the individual joke is fairly light.

jokes.text: David: 'I'm sorry, I was processing Alexis' situation.' Alexis: 'Oh burn, David.' [Moira, about Stevie:] 'A little past curfew.'
jokes.setup: David arrives home late, claiming he was out processing Alexis's supposed pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: Moira noting it's 'a little past curfew' — as if any of the Rose children have or observe curfews — and Alexis's 'Oh burn, David' at his excuse being exposed.
jokes.explanation: Moira suddenly playing the authority figure with 'a little past curfew' is funny given her general abdication of parental responsibility. Alexis's 'Oh burn' is a bit slack as dialogue but serves the sibling dynamic. Functional scene button.

jokes.text: Moira: 'David, Alexis isn't pregnant.' David: 'Okay, but just picture them as like, tiny little roommates, whose tiny little poops you get to clean up!'
jokes.setup: David is interrupted in his argument about not wanting to be involved with a baby.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn corrects the misunderstanding ('David, Alexis isn't pregnant') but David has already committed to his argument and pushes through with the 'tiny little roommates' framing — which is both absurd and revealing about his actual relationship to babies.
jokes.explanation: David's 'tiny little roommates whose tiny little poops you get to clean up' is a delightfully specific and slightly revolting image. He continues the argument even after learning it's irrelevant, which is very David. The phrase 'tiny little roommates' manages to be both endearing and dismissive simultaneously. Good character integration.

jokes.text: David: 'Um, that's a hard pass.' Stevie: 'You're gonna be so involved in their lives.' David: 'Mm, your body, your problem.'
jokes.setup: Stevie is insisting David will be involved with the twins.
jokes.punchline: David's 'your body, your problem' is a darkly absurdist dismissal — applied to a fictional pregnancy being used to torment him, with a faint echo of reproductive rights language used in a completely different context.
jokes.explanation: 'Your body, your problem' is very David — dismissive, blunt, and slightly shocking. The phrase's other cultural resonances add a layer the show probably doesn't intend as primary but are present.

tmdb_overview: Johnny braces himself for the possibility of another baby in the family after finding a positive pregnancy test and David and Patrick search for privacy.
insight: Character escalation drives 52 jokes across the hour, but 69.8 score reflects uneven landing.
